excerpt.rendered: Chasing whitewashed cliffs, sleepy donkeys, and a sunset that everyone warned would be too crowded to love.

content.rendered:
Santorini doesn't reveal itself all at once. The ferry rounds a black volcanic headland and suddenly the cliffs are there — chalk-white villages spilling over the caldera like sugar over the rim of a cup. I had read the warnings: too many cruise ships, too many influencers, too many sunsets photographed too many times. And yet, standing on the cobbled lip of Oia at half past seven, I forgot all of it.

Mornings before the crowds

The trick, as anyone who has lived on a tourist island will tell you, is to wake up before the boats do. By six the alleys belong to the bakers and the cats. I bought a bougatsa wrapped in waxed paper and ate it sitting on a low wall while the Aegean turned from slate to silver to that impossible cobalt that the postcards never quite get right.

Some places are loved into cliché. Santorini, somehow, survives the love.
— field notebook, day three

Where to actually eat

Skip the caldera-view restaurants with the laminated menus. Walk fifteen minutes inland and you'll find tavernas where the tomatoes taste like they were grown in a different decade — sweeter, denser, almost candied by the volcanic soil.

  • Metaxi Mas in Exo Gonia — book a week ahead, sit on the terrace.
  • To Psaraki down in Vlychada for whatever was caught that morning.
  • Lolita's Gelato in Oia for the salted pistachio, full stop.
  • Any bakery whose front window has a tray of melitinia at 7am.

A walk worth the blisters

The cliff-top path from Fira to Oia is ten kilometres of caldera, donkey bells, and the occasional chapel painted so blue it hurts. Start before nine, carry more water than feels reasonable, and don't trust the shade.

Logistics tip

The ferry from Athens is eight hours and beautiful for the first two. Fly in, ferry out — you'll thank yourself.

By the numbers

9
Days on the island
7
Sunsets watched
many
Glasses of Assyrtiko
11
Donkeys photographed

Leaving

I left on the late ferry, sunburnt across the bridge of my nose, my notebook smelling faintly of retsina. Santorini had been everything the brochures promised and, quietly, a great deal more.

meridian.dek: Chasing whitewashed cliffs, sleepy donkeys, and a sunset that everyone warned would be too crowded to love.

excerpt.rendered: Wet stone, paper umbrellas, and the particular hush of a temple garden when the tour buses give up and go home.

content.rendered:
It rained the entire week I spent in Kyoto, and I cannot recommend it highly enough. The city is built for weather — the deep eaves of the wooden machiya houses, the moss that drinks light off the stones at Saiho-ji, the steam curling off bowls of udon in basement noodle bars where the windows fog from the inside.

Fushimi Inari at dawn

I climbed through the vermilion gates at five in the morning, alone except for a single shrine cat who watched me with the polite suspicion of a customs officer. The torii recede uphill into mist, each one inscribed with the name of a donor, each one a small act of faith stacked on top of the last.

A garden in Kyoto is a sentence. The rain is the punctuation.

What to pack for a wet week

  • A genuinely waterproof jacket — not 'water resistant', not 'showerproof'.
  • Two pairs of shoes, so one can dry while the other works.
  • A small folding umbrella; the convenience-store ones snap by Tuesday.
  • A notebook with a hard cover. Soft covers warp.

The kissaten on a side street

Find the old coffee shops. Not the third-wave ones with the V60s and the Aesop hand soap, but the kissaten — dim, wood-panelled rooms where a man in a bow tie has been making the same siphon coffee since 1974 and where the cheesecake comes on a doily. Sit by the window. Watch the rain. Don't look at your phone.

Etiquette note

Slurp the noodles. Don't tip. Take your shoes off when the floor changes level. When in doubt, do what the person next to you is doing.

A loose itinerary

  1. Day 1 — Arrive, eat tonkatsu, sleep early.
  2. Day 2 — Fushimi Inari at dawn, Tofukuji in the afternoon.
  3. Day 3 — Philosopher's Path, end at Honen-in.
  4. Day 4 — Day trip to Uji for the matcha and the tea museum.
  5. Day 5 — Nishiki Market, then a long evening in a kissaten.

Leaving

I left Kyoto on the bullet train with damp shoes and the strong sense that I had only seen a tenth of it. That, I think, is the correct way to leave.

meridian.dek: Wet stone, paper umbrellas, and the particular hush of a temple garden when the tour buses give up and go home.

jumpLinks.text: What to pack for a wet week
The kissaten on a side street

title.rendered: Lost on Purpose in the Marrakech Souks

excerpt.rendered: A guide to surrendering your sense of direction in the world's most beautifully disorienting marketplace.

content.rendered:
There is a particular kind of peace that arrives only after you have completely abandoned the hope of finding your hotel. The Marrakech medina is engineered for this. Streets fold into streets, doorways open into courtyards that open into other courtyards, and the call to prayer ricochets off pink walls in a way that makes triangulation impossible.

The colour of everything

Saffron, indigo, tagine-orange, the deep pomegranate red of Berber rugs hung against ochre plaster — Marrakech is a city that doesn't believe in beige. The dyers' souk is the most photographed alley in the medina for a reason: skeins of wool the colour of poured paint, drying overhead like bunting.

If you can find your way out, you weren't really lost.

How to actually haggle

  1. Decide what the object is worth to you before you ask the price.
  2. Counter at roughly a third. Smile while you do it.
  3. Be willing to walk away once. Often that's when the real price appears.
  4. If you agree on a number, you buy. Walking away after is bad form.

Mint tea, properly

Accept every cup. The pour is the performance — held high so the tea aerates into the glass with a small, contented hiss. It will be sweeter than you want. Drink it anyway. Three glasses, the saying goes: the first bitter as life, the second sweet as love, the third gentle as death.

A small kindness

Learn five words of Darija before you go. 'Shukran' alone will change how a whole afternoon treats you.

What to bring home

  • A hand-hammered brass tea tray (check the rim is even).
  • Saffron from the spice souk, sealed in foil, not glass.
  • One small Beni Ourain rug, rolled tight, checked as luggage.
  • Argan oil — culinary grade, not the cosmetic stuff.

meridian.dek: A guide to surrendering your sense of direction in the world's most beautifully disorienting marketplace.
meridian.imageAlt: Lost on Purpose in the Marrakech Souks

title.rendered: Patagonia: Notes from the End of the World

excerpt.rendered: Granite spires, wind that has opinions, and the strange comfort of being somewhere genuinely indifferent to you.

content.rendered:
The wind in Patagonia is a character, not a condition. It has moods. It enters rooms. It bullies the laundry on the line behind the hostel and it changes its mind about which direction to come from with the petulance of a small monarch. I spent eleven days in El Chaltén and I never once stopped noticing it.

Walking toward Fitz Roy

The trail up to Laguna de los Tres begins as a friendly stroll through lenga forest and ends, four hours later, as a near-vertical scramble across scree that punishes everyone equally. The reward is one of the great views on the planet: the granite needles of Fitz Roy and Cerro Torre rising out of a milk-blue glacial lake, sharp as cut paper against the sky.

1,150 m
Total ascent
22 km
Round trip
9–11 hrs
Time on the trail
Always one more
Layers required
Mountains do not require you. That is the entire appeal.

Packing for the wind

  • A hard-shell jacket with a hood that actually cinches.
  • Gloves you can still tie a knot in.
  • Sunglasses — the glacier light is brutal even on grey days.
  • Twice the snacks you think you need; the wind eats calories.

The town at the trailhead

El Chaltén feels improvised, in the best way — a settlement of climbers, bakers, and dogs that all seem to know each other. The bread at La Cervecería is worth the walk across town. So is the beer. Sit on the patio when the wind drops, which it sometimes does, around the time the alpenglow hits the peaks and the whole valley goes briefly, ridiculously pink.

If you only have one day

Walk to Laguna Capri and back. Four hours, gentle, and you still get the Fitz Roy view through a frame of lenga branches.

Going home

I came home with a sunburn on one cheek, a permanently chapped lower lip, and the unshakeable conviction that everyone should, at least once, stand somewhere the wind has been blowing since long before there was anyone here to lean into it.

meridian.dek: Granite spires, wind that has opinions, and the strange comfort of being somewhere genuinely indifferent to you.
meridian.imageAlt: Patagonia: Notes from the End of the World

title.rendered: Tokyo After Dark: Neon Dreams & Late-Night Ramen

excerpt.rendered: Shinjuku alleyways, 3AM yakitori, and the electric hum of a city that never quite sleeps.

content.rendered:
Tokyo transforms after midnight. The salarymen emerge from underground stations like ghosts clocking out of a dream, and the alleyways of Shinjuku begin their second life. Golden yakitori skewers glow on charcoal grills, and the sound of ice being crushed for highballs fills the humid air.

The黄金時代 — Golden Gai

Six tiny bars squeezed into a space the size of a walk-in closet, each with seating for no more than five. The yakitori here is not a meal; it's a ritual. Chicken liver on a willow skewer, charred at the edges, served with a cold Asahi and the quiet company of strangers who may or may not speak English.

In Tokyo, you are never alone. Even in the smallest bar, someone has saved you a seat.

Ramen at 3AM

The best bowl of my life was not at a famous shop. It was in a window-less room in Kabukicho, where a man with no visible arms served tonkotsu from a cart. The broth was the colour of clouds at sunset, thick enough to cling to the noodles, and the chashu fell apart at the touch of chopsticks.

  • Ichiran — individual booths, perfect for solo eaters.
  • Gyukaku — all-you-can-eat yakiniku, with ramen for dessert.
  • Any standing bar in Omoide Yokocho — just point and smile.

Navigation tip

When lost in Shinjuku, follow the neon. The red lanterns of Omoide Yokocho appear when you need them most.

Leaving

I took the last train home at 4AM, sat in a nearly empty carriage, and watched the city lights blur past. Tokyo had shown me its secrets, and all it asked in return was that I return.

meridian.dek: Shinjuku alleyways, 3AM yakitori, and the electric hum of a city that never quite sleeps.
meridian.imageAlt: Tokyo After Dark: Neon Dreams & Late-Night Ramen

title.rendered: Iceland's Ring Road: Chasing Waterfalls in a Land That Forgot to Sleep

excerpt.rendered: Black sand beaches, thundering cataracts, and the particular silence of a midnight sun that refuses to set.

content.rendered:
The Ring Road is not a journey. It's a loop of obsession — 1,332 kilometres of geology performing for an audience of one. You drive for hours and see no one, then suddenly a waterfall appears on the horizon like a mirage made of thunder.

Seljalandsfoss & the secret cave

The famous waterfall everyone photographs is just the curtain. Walk around behind it — carefully, it's slippery — and you enter a hidden cave where the light turns green through the water. It's the kind of place that makes you believe in small magic.

Iceland doesn't have weather. It has mood swings.

The black sand

Reynisfjara beach near Vik is otherworldly — columns of basalt stacked like a giant's organ, black sand that squeaks under your feet, and waves that hit the shore with the force of a freight train. Don't turn your back on them. The sneaker waves here have killed tourists who didn't respect the water.

  • Skógafoss — climb the stairs for the aerial view.
  • Dyrhólaey — the arch rock and the lighthouse.
  • Jökulsárlón — icebergs floating like floating funeral homes.

Camping hack

Wild camping is technically illegal. But with a camper van and a parked permission, the entire country becomes yours.

The midnight sun

At 2AM, the sky was still streaked with pink. I sat on a lava field with my jacket pulled to my chin and watched the sun hover on the horizon like a tired guest who couldn't quite leave the party.

meridian.dek: Black sand beaches, thundering cataracts, and the particular silence of a midnight sun that refuses to set.
meridian.imageAlt: Iceland's Ring Road: Chasing Waterfalls in a Land That Forgot to Sleep

title.rendered: Lisbon: Riding Tram 28 Into the Heart of the Alfama

excerpt.rendered: Yellow trams, tiled facades, and the sound of fado drifting from a window you just passed.

content.rendered:
Tram 28 is a moving museum that happens to also take you somewhere useful. It rattles up the hill from Martim Moniz, through the Baixa, and into the Alfama, passing everything worth seeing in Lisbon along the way. Mind your pockets — the pickpockets are as local as the tram drivers.

The Alfama at dusk

Leave the tram at the Graça stop and walk down. The light here hits the tiled facades at a low angle that makes everything look like a painting. Every doorway promises a view, a cat, a washing line. The fado starts around eight, leaking from restaurant windows like a secret.

Lisbon is a city that forgives you for being tired. Everything waits.

Where the locals eat

  • Casa de Bacalhau — the cod is salted, dried, and perfect.
  • Mosteiro — under the Santa Luzia church, incredible views.
  • Mercado da Ribeira — the Time Out market, every cuisine represented.

Pastéis tip

The famous shop in Belém has a queue. The one in the airport, past security, has the same pasteis and zero wait.

Leaving

I left Lisbon with tile dust on my shoes and a pastel de nata sticky on my fingers. The tram bell still rings in my ears.

meridian.dek: Yellow trams, tiled facades, and the sound of fado drifting from a window you just passed.
meridian.imageAlt: Lisbon: Riding Tram 28 Into the Heart of the Alfama

title.rendered: Bali: Temple Days & Rice Terrace Haze

excerpt.rendered: Offerings on the sidewalk, Sacred Monkey Forest, and the particular green of a terraced hillside at dawn.

content.rendered:
Bali doesn't wait for you to be ready. The offerings appear at dawn — little woven baskets of flowers and rice placed on doorsteps, at shrines, on the sidewalk outside your villa. The island breathes ritual like other places breathe air.

Tirta Empul

The holy water temple where Balinese Hindus come to bathe. You join the queue, receive a sarong, and move through thirty fountains, each with its own purpose. Blessing. Purification. Release. The water is cold enough to make you gasp, and warm enough to make you stay.

In Bali, the sacred and the daily share the same address.

Tegallalang at dawn

The rice terraces are a tourist trap by noon — rented swings, photographers, traffic. But at six in the morning, they're something else entirely. Mist clings to the terraces, the farmers are already working, and the green is so vivid it hurts your eyes in the best way.

  • Sacred Monkey Forest — don't wear anything shiny.
  • Goa Gajah — the elephant cave, carved into a cliff.
  • Tanah Lot — sunset, crowds, but worth it anyway.

Sarbung

Wrap a sarong around your waist before entering any temple. They rent them at the entrance for a few thousand rupiah.

Leaving

I left Ubud with a small bronze bell from a workshop near the palace and the smell of frangipani still in my hair.

meridian.dek: Offerings on the sidewalk, Sacred Monkey Forest, and the particular green of a terraced hillside at dawn.
meridian.imageAlt: Bali: Temple Days & Rice Terrace Haze

title.rendered: The Dolomites: Via Ferrata Above the Clouds

excerpt.rendered: Iron rungs, via ferratas, and a lunch of speck and apples eaten on a ledge 3,000 metres up.

content.rendered:
The Dolomites are not like the Alps. They are pale, almost phosphorescent, rising out of the mist like a city made of bone. The via ferratas here are serious — cables bolted to vertical limestone, iron rungs cemented into the rock, and a harness that you'll either love or hate.

Lagazuoi and the tunnel war

You can take a cable car up to 2,700 metres and then walk through a First World War tunnel that soldiers carved into the mountain. The graffiti they left behind is more honest than any museum.

The Dolomites don't invite you. They demand you.

The food at the rifugio

Every mountain hut serves the same three things: pasta e fagioli, speck with bread, and apple strudel. It sounds simple until you've earned it. Until you've climbed for four hours and the strudel arrives warm, with cream, and the entire valley is below you.

  • Cinque Torri — easiest via ferrata, incredible views.
  • Tofana di Rozes — harder, more exposed, unforgettable.
  • Sass de Stria — easy access, historical significance.

Gear check

Harness and via ferrata kit are non-negotiable. Rent in Cortina — the shops are well-stocked and the staff know the routes.

Leaving

I left Cortina with mud on my knees, a bruise on my shoulder from the harness, and the absolute certainty that I would return.

meridian.dek: Iron rungs, via ferratas, and a lunch of speck and apples eaten on a ledge 3,000 metres up.
meridian.imageAlt: The Dolomites: Via Ferrata Above the Clouds

title.rendered: Hanoi: A Street Food Tour Through the Old Quarter

excerpt.rendered: Bun cha at a plastic table, egg coffee in a hidden café, and the chaos of scooters filling every gap.

content.rendered:
Hanoi is a city that eats standing up. There is no seated restaurant experience here — there are plastic stools on sidewalks, women stirring pots over charcoal, and the constant, beautiful noise of a city that has been feeding itself on the street for a thousand years.

The bun cha run

Bun cha is the dish. Rice noodles, grilled pork, herbs, broth. The famous shop near Hoan Kiem Lake has a line that moves fast. You sit, you eat, you leave. The bill is about thirty thousand dong. The taste is about ten thousand times that.

In Hanoi, a meal is measured in minutes, not dollars.

Egg coffee

Cà phê trứng — beaten egg yolk and condensed coffee, layered in a glass. It sounds wrong. It is spectacular. The original shop in the Old Quarter is hidden down an alley so narrow you have to turn sideways to find it.

  • Phở 10 Ly — twenty-four-hour pho, legendary.
  • Banh da cua — crab dumpling soup, blue-shell building.
  • Cha ca — grilled fish with dill, the dish that defined a street.

Crossing the street

Don't wait for a gap. Walk slowly and steadily. The scooters will go around you. They're more afraid of you than you are of them.

Leaving

I left Hanoi with coffee grounds on my shirt and the taste of fish sauce on my fingers. It was perfect.

meridian.dek: Bun cha at a plastic table, egg coffee in a hidden café, and the chaos of scooters filling every gap.
meridian.imageAlt: Hanoi: A Street Food Tour Through the Old Quarter

title.rendered: Petra by Night: Candles, Rose City, and Silence

excerpt.rendered: Walking through the Siq by candlelight to see the Treasury glowing in the dark.

content.rendered:
Petra by night is not a tour. It's a ritual. You walk eight hundred metres through the Siq — the narrow canyon that serves as the city's entrance — by the light of a thousand candles stuck into the sandstone walls. You don't talk. Nobody does.

The Treasury appears

You emerge from the Siq and the Treasury is there, carved into the rose-red cliff face, lit from below by candles that make it glow like something from a dream. Someone plays a flute. Someone else offers tea. You sit on the ground and watch the candles flicker.

Petra doesn't reveal itself. It remembers itself, and lets you witness.

The day after

Return at sunrise. The Siq in daylight is a completely different place — colours in the rock you couldn't see at night, vendors setting up their stalls, the first Bedouin with a donkey walking past. The Monastery is worth the climb — eight hundred steps, but the view from the top is the real Petra.

  • The Treasury — go early morning or late afternoon.
  • The Monastery — same, harder climb.
  • The High Place of Sacrifice — the best sunset spot.

Book ahead

Petra by Night only runs on Monday, Wednesday, and Thursday. Book at the visitor centre or your hotel.

Leaving

I left Petra with sand in my shoes and candle wax on my fingers, and I understood why the Nabataeans chose this place.

meridian.dek: Walking through the Siq by candlelight to see the Treasury glowing in the dark.
meridian.imageAlt: Petra by Night: Candles, Rose City, and Silence

title.rendered: Machu Picchu: Above the Clouds, Below the Sun

excerpt.rendered: Inca ruins, altitude headaches, and the moment the mist parts to reveal the citadel.

content.rendered:
You can take the train to Machu Picchu. Everyone does. But if you want to understand the place, you should walk the Inca Trail — four days of altitude, Inca steps, and a final dawn that will make you forget every step was hard.

The Sun Gate

Inti Punku, the Sun Gate, is where you see Machu Picchu for the first time. You arrive at dawn, exhausted, cold, and then the mist shifts and there's the citadel, sitting on its ridge like it grew there. It did, more or less.

The Incas didn't build below the clouds. They built where the clouds lived.

The trail

The four-day trek is not easy. Altitude sickness is real. The highest pass is 4,215 metres. The stairs are carved from stone and they do not care about your knees. But the camps are beautiful, the guides are incredible, and the final morning makes every step worth it.

  • Day 1 — Cusco to Wayllabamba, easy.
  • Day 2 — Dead Woman's Pass, the hardest day.
  • Day 3 — Ruinari and the Cloud Forest.
  • Day 4 — Sunrise at Inti Punku, the citadel.

Book now

The Inca Trail permits are limited and sell out months ahead. Book through a licensed operator.

Leaving

I left Machu Picchu on the train back to Cusco, watching the mountains disappear into cloud, already planning my return.

meridian.dek: Inca ruins, altitude headaches, and the moment the mist parts to reveal the citadel.
meridian.imageAlt: Machu Picchu: Above the Clouds, Below the Sun

title.rendered: The Amalfi Coast: Lemon Groves and Curves in the Road

excerpt.rendered: Cliffside towns, driving the SS163, and the particular blue of the Tyrrhenian Sea.

content.rendered:
The Amalfi Coast is a road — technically the SS163 — that was carved into cliffs by someone who clearly thought the Italians needed a reason to make the drive every day. It's narrow, there are tunnels that seem to go on forever, and the views are so relentless you'll forget to watch the road.

Ravello

Above the chaos of Amalfi town, Ravello is quiet. The Villa Cimbrone gardens have a viewpoint called the Terrace of the Infinity, and it's not hyperbole. The sea stretches out so far it becomes the sky.

The Amalfi Coast isn't a destination. It's a state of looking.

The lemons

Everything here is made with lemons. Limoncello, lemon cake, lemon pasta, lemon gelato. The groves cling to the cliffs like stubborn thoughts. The best limoncello is the one made by someone's grandmother — you won't find it in a shop.

  • Da Adolfo — beach access, the best lunch.
  • Il Ritrovo — in Montepertuso, incredible pasta.
  • La Sponda — in Positano, wine by the candle.

Driving

If you can, avoid August. And if someone honks at you, it's not road rage. It's just how they say 'good morning' here.

Leaving

I left Positano with lemon oil on my hands and a head full of impossible blues.

meridian.dek: Cliffside towns, driving the SS163, and the particular blue of the Tyrrhenian Sea.
meridian.imageAlt: The Amalfi Coast: Lemon Groves and Curves in the Road

title.rendered: Cappadocia at Sunrise: Balloons Over the Fairy Chimneys

excerpt.rendered: Hot air balloons at dawn, cave hotels, and the strange landscape that looks like Mars but feels like home.

content.rendered:
You wake up at four in the morning because that's what this place demands. You're not a tourist in Cappadocia — you're a pilgrim. The balloons don't just fly; they rise, one by one, like thoughts floating up from the earth.

The balloon ride

One hour in the air, over the fairy chimneys — those towering volcanic cones that look like something Dr. Seuss designed after a nightmare. The pilots are experts, finding thermal currents that keep you aloft without burning too much fuel. You land in a field, drink champagne, and become someone who has seen the sunrise from above.

Cappadocia is the kind of place that makes you believe in geology as poetry.

Underground cities

The region is riddled with underground cities — Derinkuyu, Kaymakli — where early Christians hid from Roman armies. You go down eight levels, through narrow tunnels, and emerge hours later with a new appreciation for underground living.

  • Hot air balloon — book the premium companies.
  • Derinkuyu — the deepest underground city.
  • Sunrise hike in Göreme — free, equally magical.

Timing

March and November have fewer balloons in the sky but better hotel prices and fewer crowds.

Leaving

I left Cappadocia with a pocket full of travertine dust and a memory card full of balloons.

meridian.dek: Hot air balloons at dawn, cave hotels, and the strange landscape that looks like Mars but feels like home.
meridian.imageAlt: Cappadocia at Sunrise: Balloons Over the Fairy Chimneys
